summary: Generative coding tools handle boilerplate, tests, and documentation on many teams, but shipping production software still requires architecture decisions, debugging novel failures, and accountability for security and compliance. Evidence is mixed on net hiring effects; the clearer trend is a shift in task mix toward review, integration, and problem framing rather than line-by-line typing alone.

event.label: Generative coding tools
event.description: Large language models enter mainstream development workflows for code completion and review assistance.

summary: No-code builders and AI layout tools speed simple marketing sites, yet custom applications still need performance tuning, accessibility compliance, authentication, and maintenance across browsers. Pressure is strongest on template-heavy freelance work, less on product engineering roles inside organizations.

The classic programmer task of translating a fixed spec into code faces automation pressure, especially for well-defined routines. Demand remains for legacy maintenance, embedded systems, and verifying that machine-generated code meets safety and regulatory requirements.

Headcount has fallen for decades as OCR, ERP integrations, and robotic process automation absorbed keyboard entry. Recent document-AI tools extend that trend into semi-structured forms and invoice parsing. Remaining work tends to be exception handling, verification, and legacy systems without clean APIs — not a new occupation, but a shrinking slice of office labor.

event.label: RPA in back offices
event.description: Robotic process automation targets repetitive form and invoice entry.

summary: Automated analyzers and machine-assisted image review speed throughput in some labs. Specimen collection, calibration, contamination control, and explaining equivocal results to clinicians remain hands-on, regulated duties that limit end-to-end automation.

Hand drafting ended long before generative AI; CAD and BIM already reshaped the job. Today's change is incremental — AI-assisted clash detection and generative layout suggestions — layered onto an occupation already renamed and merged with engineering technician work in many firms.

Image-generation models compress mood-board and stock-style production timelines and undercut some commodity illustration pricing. Client work that needs consistent characters, legal clearance, brand ownership, and art direction still largely employs human illustrators, but the freelance market for quick-turnaround visuals has tightened noticeably since 2022.

sources.title: U.S. Copyright Office — AI and copyright

summary: Transcript-based assembly, auto-sync, and AI-assisted color tools reduce logistical grunt work in post. Story rhythm, performance choices, and director collaboration remain human-led in feature and long-form television; pressure is uneven between social clips and theatrical release work.

Writers' rooms may experiment with model-generated outlines, but credited screen work on union productions still flows through human authorship, guild rules, and showrunner approval. Documented displacement is limited; the more visible effect is faster ideation in early development, not fewer employed writers on greenlit projects.

Synthetic voice tools pressure phone-tree narration, low-budget e-learning, and some localization pickups. Character performance, live-directed sessions, and union contract limits on digital replicas have become central battlegrounds — displacement is real in commodity tiers, contested in premium tiers.

event.label: Synthetic voice in media
event.description: Cloned and generated voices spread in games, ads, and audiobooks, prompting guild responses.

summary: Grammar and summarization tools accelerate first-pass cleanup. Publishers and newsrooms still employ editors for structure, legal review, voice, and accountability — especially where errors carry reputational or regulatory cost. Commodity web copy is more exposed than investigative or book editing.

Computational capture and generative fill reduce retouch time and compete with generic stock libraries. Wedding, news, sports, forensic, and on-location brand work still require a photographer present; the squeeze is strongest on studio product and royalty-free asset markets.

Newsroom employment fell for years because of digital advertising and platform shifts — trends that predate ChatGPT. Generative tools now accelerate wire-style summaries and translation, adding pressure on commodity briefs while investigative and local beats still depend on sourcing and editorial judgment. Attributing the whole decline to AI alone would overstate the case.

sources.title: Pew Research Center — U.S. newsroom employment

summary: Style-matched image models compete with quick editorial cartoons and social gag art. Syndicated strips, graphic novels, and distinctive long-running characters still anchor human cartooning careers, but turnaround commissions face more price pressure than a decade ago.

Digital doubles and de-aging change some stunt and background economics, yet live theatre, union feature work, and audience preference for recognizable performers limit whole-role replacement in high-budget production. Evidence of net unemployment from AI specifically remains thin compared with broader streaming-era labor disputes.

Tax and bookkeeping software absorbed arithmetic decades ago; AI now speeds reconciliation matching and anomaly flags. Licensed accountants still sign opinions, advise on complex entities, and carry liability — the occupation contracts at the bookkeeping fringe more than at the CPA core.

Cloud ledgers that auto-import bank feeds and categorize transactions have reduced standalone bookkeeping headcount since the 2010s. AI categorization improves further on routine receipts. Surviving roles cluster around payroll exceptions, multi-entity cleanup, and client support.

The traditional secretary role shrank with email, calendars, and executive self-service long before generative AI. Modern administrative assistants survive where logistics, stakeholder coordination, and discretion matter; pure typing-and-filing secretarial pools are already rare in large offices.

Workflow automation routes routine filings in government and corporate records. Clerks who handle exceptions, in-person service, court procedure, and retention rules remain necessary; automation tends to thin routine back-office tiers rather than eliminate public-facing clerk roles entirely.

Analytics and AI sampling let audit teams test more transactions per engagement. Signing auditors still interview management, evaluate controls, and accept professional liability — automation widens coverage more than it removes the licensed role.

Robo-advisors commoditized simple portfolio allocation years ago. Human advisors persist where estate planning, tax complexity, and behavioral coaching require trust and fiduciary conversation; AI tools mostly speed research prep behind those meetings.

Generative copy and image tools lower the cost of campaign variants. Managers still own budget allocation, brand consistency, channel strategy, and interpreting performance data — documented job losses tied specifically to generative AI in marketing leadership remain limited.

Contract review and legal research models accelerate document-heavy practice areas. Courtroom advocacy, client counseling, negotiation, and bar licensure keep attorneys accountable; early studies suggest time savings on discovery more than wholesale replacement of associates across all practice types.

Ambient documentation and decision-support tools change how clinicians spend time, often reducing charting burden. Diagnosis, procedure consent, and medical liability remain human; AI assists documentation and triage suggestions rather than replacing licensed practice in mainstream care settings.

Automated dispensing and interaction checks handle verification volume in many chains. Pharmacists of record still counsel patients, manage therapy, and administer vaccines where regulations require a licensed pharmacist on site.

AI triage may flag scans for radiologist priority, but patient positioning, radiation safety, and equipment operation require credentialed technologists in the room. Automation changes workflow speed more than it removes the technologist role.

Adaptive software and homework assistants shift some drill practice outside class. In-person teaching still centers on classroom management, safeguarding, motivation, and social learning — areas poorly automated. Policy fights over student use of chatbots are visible; mass teacher layoffs attributed solely to AI are not well documented in K–12 systems.

Catalog search digitization reduced pure retrieval work years ago. Librarians increasingly teach information literacy — including how to evaluate AI-generated answers — and run community programs; employment trends vary by municipality more than by a single AI displacement curve.

Grading aids and literature summarizers change preparation time for some faculty. Tenure-track hiring still rewards original research and seminar teaching; institutions are revising authorship and plagiarism policies rather than eliminating professorial lines because of models alone.

Step-by-step solvers and on-demand apps compete with homework help. Human tutors retain demand for accountability, exam strategy, special-needs support, and subjects where reasoning must be spoken aloud — but commodity subject tutoring faces more online competition than before.

Generative massing and environmental simulation speed early design iterations. Stamping drawings, code compliance, and client coordination still require licensed architects who carry legal responsibility for built work.
Simulation and digital twins improve planning models. Sign-off on safety factors, geotechnical judgment, and construction oversight remains a licensed engineering duty with public accountability.

Self-checkout and mobile payment reduced cashier hours in supermarkets over the past decade — a trend that started before generative AI. Computer-vision checkout extends automation in some chains; attendants increasingly supervise kiosks and verify age-restricted sales rather than scan every item.

event.label: Self-checkout expansion
event.description: Supermarket self-scan lanes become standard in many markets.

summary: Online quote engines handle simple personal lines. Agents remain common in commercial insurance, life underwriting nuance, and claims advocacy where policyholders expect a named contact.

Listing portals and automated valuations changed marketing mechanics years ago. Transactions still involve negotiation, disclosure law, and local knowledge; commission structures face political pressure independent of AI.

Mobile check-in and messaging concierges reduced front-desk volume in mid-scale hotels before generative chatbots. AI assistants extend that trend for routine questions; in-person staff remain for complaints, security issues, and high-touch arrivals.

Online booking displaced most commodity itinerary shopping by the 2000s. AI trip planners continue self-service trends; surviving agencies focus on complex multi-stop travel, corporate policy, and rebooking during disruptions — a smaller profession than its peak, for reasons broader than generative AI alone.

Trend analytics and generative print tools speed mood-board work. Seasonal collections still depend on fit testing, sourcing relationships, and human taste calls on the runway; documented designer unemployment from AI specifically is limited.

AutoML accelerates prototype modeling. Experimental design, causal inference, regulatory submissions, and communicating uncertainty to decision-makers still require statisticians — automation raises output per analyst more than it eliminates the occupation.
